perturbation.original_text: WHEREAS, PCQ and ABW have executed that certain Co-Branding Agreement dated October 11, 1996, as amended on December 10, 1996 (as so amended, the "Agreement"); WHEREAS, the parties hereto desire to further amend the Agreement to modify the provisions of the Agreement regarding [***] of the term thereof; and WHEREAS, the parties hereto desire to set forth herein the terms and conditions of their agreements and understandings with respect to the foregoing.
perturbation.changed_text: WHEREAS, PCQ and ABW have executed that certain Co-Branding Agreement dated October 11, 1996, as amended on December 10, 1996 (as so amended, the "Agreement"); WHEREAS, the parties hereto desire to further amend the Agreement to modify the provisions of the Agreement regarding [***] of the term thereof; and WHEREAS, the parties hereto desire to set forth herein the terms and conditions of their agreements and understandings with respect to the foregoing. This agreement shall have a term of 1 year, automatically renewing for successive 1-year terms unless either party provides notice of non-renewal within 15 days of the end of the then-current term.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text shortens the notification period for non-renewal to 15 days. This creates an inconsistency because depending on the state, this may be considered an unreasonably short period to provide notice of non-renewal, potentially conflicting with legal standards of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: The implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing requires parties to a contract to act honestly and reasonably. A 15-day notice period for non-renewal of a contract, especially in complex co-branding agreements, could be seen as unreasonably short, violating the implied covenant by not providing sufficient time for the other party to make alternative arrangements or protect their interests.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a 15-day notice period for non-renewal, which the Law Explanation states might be 'unreasonably short, violating the implied covenant' requiring parties to act 'honestly and reasonably.' This inconsistency, as defined in the instructions, is a 'time-sensitive obligation' that may not align with legal standards, potentially conflicting with the 'obligation of good faith' as mentioned in Scraped Law Snippet 1, which says 'Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' Therefore, the change creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The preamble hereto is incorporated herein and, by this reference, is made a substantive part hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: The preamble hereto is incorporated herein and, by this reference, is made a substantive part hereof. Any disputes arising out of or relating to this agreement must be resolved through mandatory arbitration, with the arbitrator selected solely by PCQ.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text introduces a mandatory arbitration clause where the arbitrator is selected solely by one party (PCQ). This is likely unenforceable and unconscionable under many state laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Arbitration Act & State contract law on unconscionability.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the FAA favors arbitration, clauses that are unconscionable (grossly unfair or one-sided) are generally unenforceable. Allowing one party to unilaterally select the arbitrator raises serious concerns about bias and fairness, potentially rendering the arbitration agreement invalid under state contract law principles of unconscionability.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a mandatory arbitration clause where the arbitrator is selected solely by one party (PCQ). The Law Explanation says that 'Allowing one party to unilaterally select the arbitrator raises serious concerns about bias and fairness, potentially rendering the arbitration agreement invalid under state contract law principles of unconscionability.' Scraped Law Snippet 1, 9 USC 2, discusses the validity and enforcement of arbitration agreements. While the FAA generally favors arbitration, the Law Explanation points out that clauses that are unconscionable are generally unenforceable. Because the modified clause allows one party to unilaterally select the arbitrator, it creates an inconsistency that could render the arbitration agreement invalid, thus creating a contradiction with the legal principles governing arbitration agreements.

perturbation.original_text: Except as modified hereby, the Agreement and its terms and provisions are hereby ratified and confirmed for all purposes and in all respects.
perturbation.changed_text: Except as modified hereby, the Agreement and its terms and provisions are hereby ratified and confirmed for all purposes and in all respects. PCQ shall have the right to terminate this agreement at any time, for any reason, with no notice required.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text grants PCQ the right to terminate the agreement at any time, for any reason, without notice. This potentially conflicts with the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, as well as state laws regarding reasonable notice for contract termination.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing and State Contract Law
perturbation.law_explanation: The implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing prevents parties from exercising their contractual rights in a way that unfairly harms the other party. The right to terminate at any time, for any reason, without notice, could be considered a violation of this covenant if exercised in bad faith, particularly if ABW has made substantial investments or reliance on the agreement. Many states also have laws requiring reasonable notice for contract termination, even if the contract doesn't explicitly state it.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a termination clause that allows PCQ to terminate the agreement at any time, for any reason, without notice. This contradicts the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, as well as potential state laws requiring reasonable notice for contract termination. The Law Explanation highlights this conflict, noting that such a termination right could be considered a violation of the covenant if exercised in bad faith, especially if ABW has made substantial investments or relied on the agreement. While Scraped Law Snippets provided do not address this issue directly, the Law Explanation strongly suggests a contradiction, in that such a clause could be deemed unlawful under established legal principles if deployed unfairly.
